subject: Physics
chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: When a sine wave travels through a region, two particles $A$ and $B$ exhibit a phase difference of $\pi$.
options: The displacements of $A$ and $B$ possess equal magnitudes.
Particles $A$ and $B$ move in opposite directions.
Particle $A$ oscillates at half the frequency of particle $B$.
The separation between $A$ and $B$ must be exactly half of the wavelength.
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the traveling sine wave be $y = A \sin(\omega t - kx)$.

The phase of a particle at position $x$ is $\phi = \omega t - kx$. For particles $A$ and $B$ with a phase difference of $\pi$, we have $\phi_A - \phi_B = \pi$.

The displacements of the particles are:
$y_A = A \sin(\phi_A)$
$y_B = A \sin(\phi_B) = A \sin(\phi_A - \pi) = -A \sin(\phi_A) = -y_A$
Since $y_B = -y_A$, the magnitudes of their displacements are always equal ($|y_A| = |y_B|$).

The velocities of the particles are:
$v_A = \dfrac{dy_A}{dt} = A\omega \cos(\phi_A)$
$v_B = \dfrac{dy_B}{dt} = A\omega \cos(\phi_A - \pi) = -A\omega \cos(\phi_A) = -v_A$
Since $v_B = -v_A$, the particles always move in opposite directions.

Both particles are excited by the same wave, so they oscillate with the same frequency. Thus, the frequency of $A$ is equal to the frequency of $B$.

The phase difference is related to the path difference by $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$. For the particles to be in opposite phase (equivalent to a phase difference of $\pi$, $3\pi$, $5\pi$, etc.), the separation can be $\Delta x = \left(n + \dfrac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$. Therefore, the separation does not have to be exactly half of the wavelength; it can be any odd multiple of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A mechanical wave travels in a medium along the $X$-axis. The particles of the medium
options: must move along the $X$-axis
must move along the $Y$-axis
may move along the $X$-axis
may move along the $Y$-axis
questions.solution:
A mechanical wave can be either longitudinal or transverse.

For a longitudinal wave travelling along the $X$-axis, the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of propagation, which is along the $X$-axis.

For a transverse wave travelling along the $X$-axis, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of propagation, which can be along the $Y$-axis or $Z$-axis.

Thus, the particles may move along the $X$-axis or may move along the $Y$-axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A longitudinal wave is unable to
options: possess a unique wave velocity
undergo polarization
possess a unique wavelength
transfer energy
questions.solution:
Polarization is a phenomenon restricted to transverse waves, where the direction of oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.

In longitudinal waves, the particles of the medium oscillate along the direction of wave propagation. Since there is only one axis of oscillation, longitudinal waves cannot be polarized.

Like all waves, longitudinal waves transfer energy and can possess a unique wave velocity and wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
options: every antinode oscillates in phase
all particles located between consecutive nodes oscillate in phase
alternate antinodes oscillate in phase
every particle of the medium oscillates in phase
questions.solution:
The equation of a stationary wave can be written as $y = A \sin(kx) \cos(\omega t)$.

The term $A \sin(kx)$ gives the amplitude of oscillation for a particle at position $x$.

Between two consecutive nodes, the value of $kx$ lies in the interval $(n\pi, (n+1)\pi)$. In this region, the sign of $\sin(kx)$ remains the same. Hence, all particles located between two consecutive nodes oscillate in phase.

When crossing a node, the sign of $\sin(kx)$ changes, which means particles in adjacent loops oscillate out of phase by $\pi$.

Since the sign of $\sin(kx)$ alternates for consecutive loops, particles in alternate loops (and thus alternate antinodes) have the same sign and oscillate in phase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
options: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a travelling wave
The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a standing wave
The energies of all the small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave
The energies of all the small parts of equal length are equal in a standing wave
questions.solution:
In a travelling wave, energy is continuously transmitted along the string.

The energy of a small part of the string in a travelling wave fluctuates with time, as its kinetic and potential energy densities are maximum at the mean position and zero at the extreme position. Hence, option 1 is incorrect.

Since the energy density is non-uniform at any given instant, all small parts of equal length do not possess equal energies in a travelling wave. Hence, option 3 is incorrect.

In a standing wave, energy is confined within regions and no net energy is transmitted along the string. Each small part of the string executes simple harmonic motion with a fixed amplitude depending on its position.

For a particle executing simple harmonic motion, the total energy is constant. Therefore, the energy of any small part of the string remains constant in a standing wave. Hence, option 2 is correct.

In a standing wave, the amplitude of oscillation varies with position, being zero at the nodes and maximum at the antinodes. Since the total energy of an oscillator depends on the square of its amplitude, small parts of equal length located at different positions will have different energies. Hence, option 4 is incorrect.
